title: myTax 2025 Seniors and pensioners (includes self-funded retirees)
description: How to determine your eligibility for the seniors and pensioners tax offset when lodging your tax return using myTax.
keywords: 2025, myTax, e-tax, online tax return, seniors, pensioners, self-funded retiree

content_blocks.text: Things to know
Complete this section if you're eligible for the seniors and pensioners tax offset (SAPTO), as it may reduce your tax liability.
To qualify for this tax offset, you must meet both conditions:

items: eligibility for Australian Government pensions and similar payments
rebate income amounts

content_blocks.text: If you had a spouse at any time during 2024–25, you:

items: also need to work out whether they're eligible for the tax offset
may not get the SAPTO even if you meet all the eligibility conditions, as we base the amount of the tax offset on your individual rebate income, not your combined rebate income.

content_blocks.text: We'll use the information you provide in this section to calculate your tax offset. If you have a spouse that is also eligible for this tax offset, we'll automatically transfer any unused amounts between you.
If you're in jail for the whole of 2024–25, you can't claim this tax offset.
Completing this section
We may have pre-filled your tax offset code for you based on the information you entered into myTax.
At Personalise return , you don’t need to make a selection to show seniors and pensioners as it's always displayed at Prepare return when you:

items: have shown an amount at Australian Government payments such as pensions and carer payments
are 60 years old or older.

content_blocks.text: To show your seniors and pensioners tax offset code, at Prepare return select 'Add/Edit' at the Offsets banner.
At the Seniors and pensioners (includes self-funded retirees) heading:

items: Has the Tax offset code pre-filled in myTax? Yes – go to step 2 . No – go to step 3 .
Is the pre-filled code correct for your circumstances? If unsure, check Tax offset codes table. Yes – go to step 4 . No – go to step 3 .
If you meet both of the eligibility conditions , choose the tax offset code that applies to you in the Tax offset codes table.
Were you or your spouse a veteran, war widow or war widower? No – go to step 5 . Yes – choose the veteran code that applies to you in the Veteran codes table. Go to step 5 .
Go back to myTax, which should be open in another tab or window. Select your Tax offset code and Veteran code (if applicable) from the drop-down lists.
Select Save and continue when you have completed the Offsets section.

content_blocks.text: Tax offset codes
Work out which of the following SAPTO code letters applied to your circumstances at any time during 2024–25.

rows: Your eligibility and circumstance at any time in 2024–25
You met the eligibility conditions for this tax offset and were single, separated or widowed.
You and your spouse: both met the eligibility conditions for this tax offset, and had to live apart due to illness or live apart because one of you was in a nursing home.
You met the eligibility conditions for this tax offset, but your spouse didn't, and you and your spouse either had to live apart due to illness live apart because one of you was in a nursing home.
You and your spouse: lived together, and both met the eligibility conditions for this tax offset.
You and your spouse: lived together, and you met the eligibility conditions for this tax offset, but your spouse didn't.

content_blocks.text: If more than one code applies, use the first code in the list above that applies to you, except:

items: if both A and B apply, use code A if your spouse's taxable income was $18,334 or more. Otherwise, use code B .
if both A and D apply, use code A if your spouse's taxable income was $12,494 or more. Otherwise, use code D .

content_blocks.text: Veteran codes
If you or your spouse is a veteran, war widow or war widowers, select the veteran code letters that applies to you.

rows: You're a veteran, war widow or war widower.
Your spouse was a veteran, war widow or war widower.
Both you and your spouse were a veteran, war widow or war widower.

content_blocks.text: Eligibility conditions
To claim this offset, you must meet condition 1 and condition 2. If you meet both conditions, go to Completing this section .
Eligibility for Australian Government pensions and similar payments
Condition 1: You meet this condition if you can answer Yes to any of the following in 2024–25.

rows: At Australian Government payments such as pensions and carer payments in your tax return, you included either: a pension or allowance from Centrelink a pension, allowance or benefit from the Department of Veterans' Affairs (DVA). Visit Australian Government payments such as pensions and carer payments to learn more. Note: If you're under the age-pension age : Australian Government payments aren't taxable in some circumstances. When these payments aren't taxable, don't include them at Australian Government payments such as pensions and carer payments . If your Australian Government payments aren't taxable, they don't meet this eligibility condition. Visit Amounts that you do not pay tax on to learn more.

You met the Centrelink age pension age requirement and were eligible for an Australian Government age pension during 2024–25, but didn't receive it because: you didn't make a claim you didn't meet the income test or the assets test, but you satisfied one of the following you had been an Australian resident for age-pension purposes for 10 years or more, of which 5 years of these years were continuous you had a qualifying residence exemption because you arrived in Australia as a refugee or under a special humanitarian program you received a partner allowance, a widow allowance or a widow B pension, immediately before turning age pension age you're a woman who was widowed in Australia, and you and your late partner were Australian residents when your late partner died you're currently in Australia you have been an Australian resident for at least the last 2 years you're a woman and on 19 March 2020 you were either receiving a wife pension and you weren't receiving a carer allowance; or a special needs widow B pension you're a woman and on 31 December 2021 you were receiving a widow allowance you would have qualified for an age pension under an international social security agreement.

You met the veteran pension age test and were eligible for a pension, allowance or benefit from Veterans' Affairs during 2024–25, but didn't receive it because: you didn't make a claim, or you didn't meet the income test or the assets test, and you're either a veteran with eligible war service a Commonwealth veteran, allied veteran or allied mariner with qualifying service.

content_blocks.text: If you:

items: answer Yes to any of the condition 1 requirements, continue to condition 2 .
answer No to all the condition 1 requirements, you're not eligible for this tax offset.

content_blocks.text: Centrelink age pension age
To be eligible for an Australian Government age pension from Centrelink, on 30 June 2025 you must be 67 years or older External Link .
To work out whether you're eligible for a social security or Centrelink pension only, go to the Services Australia website External Link or contact Services Australia External Link .
Veteran pension age test
To be eligible for a pension, allowance or benefit from Veterans' Affairs you must:

items: be 60 years old or older on 30 June 2025, and
meet the veteran pension age test.

content_blocks.text: You meet the veteran pension age test if one of the following applied to you, and you're eligible for a pension, allowance or benefit under the Veterans' Entitlements Act 1986 :

items: you have eligible war service, that is, service in World War II or operational service as a member of the Australian Defence Force

you're a Commonwealth or allied veteran who served in a conflict in which the Australian Defence Force was engaged during a period of hostilities that is, World War II, Korea, Malaya, Indonesia or Vietnam
you're an Australian or allied mariner who served during World War II
you're the war widow or widower of a former member of the Australian Defence Force.

content_blocks.text: Pension, allowance or benefit includes:

items: disability pension
service pension
white or gold Repatriation health cards for treatment entitlements.

content_blocks.text: If you're not sure whether you're eligible for a payment, go to the Department of Veterans' Affairs website External Link or contact the Department of Veterans' Affairs External Link .
Rebate income amounts
Condition 2: You meet this condition if you can answer Yes to any of the following for 2024–25.

rows: You didn't have a spouse, and your rebate income was less than $52,759.
You had a spouse, and the combined rebate income of you and your spouse was less than $87,620.

At any time in 2024–25 both of the following apply you and your spouse had to live apart due to illness or because one of you was in a nursing home the combined rebate income of you and your spouse was less than $100,104.

content_blocks.text: If you:

items: answer Yes to any of the condition 2 requirements, go to Completing this section .
answer No to all the condition 2 requirements, you're not eligible for this tax offset.

content_blocks.text: Rebate income
Your ‘rebate income' is used to determine whether you're eligible for the seniors and pensioners tax offset.

If you have a spouse, your combined rebate income is used to work out whether you're eligible for this tax offset, but your own rebate income is used to work out the amount you're entitled to. Depending on how much income you have earned, you could meet the overall eligibility requirements and still not get this tax offset.
Use the following worksheet if you want to know how to work out your:

items: rebate income
combined rebate income (if you have a spouse).

rows: Assessable first home super saver (FHSS) released amount
Take away the amount at row b from a . If the amount is less than zero, write 0 at row c . The result is your taxable income disregarding your assessable FHSS released amount.
Reportable fringe benefits amounts from employers exempt from FBT under section 57A of FBTAA 1986 multiplied by 0.53. Round down the answer to the nearest dollar.
Reportable fringe benefits amounts from employers not exempt from FBT under section 57A of FBTAA 1986.
Add up the amounts from rows c to i . This is each individual's rebate income.

The amount on which a trustee of a trust was liable to pay tax in respect of your spouse because your spouse was under a legal disability, such as: being an undischarged bankrupt a person who was declared legally incapable because of a mental condition.
Add the amount from: row j in your column row j in your spouse's column, and row k in your spouse's column. This is your combined rebate income.

content_text:
## Things to know
Complete this section if you're eligible for the seniors and pensioners tax offset (SAPTO), as it may reduce your tax liability.
To qualify for this tax offset, you must meet both conditions:
- eligibility for Australian Government pensions and similar payments
- rebate income amounts
If you had a spouse at any time during 2024–25, you:
- also need to work out whether they're eligible for the tax offset
- may not get the SAPTO even if you meet all the eligibility conditions, as we base the amount of the tax offset on your individual rebate income, not your combined rebate income.
We'll use the information you provide in this section to calculate your tax offset. If you have a spouse that is also eligible for this tax offset, we'll automatically transfer any unused amounts between you.
If you're in jail for the whole of 2024–25, you can't claim this tax offset.
## Completing this section
We may have pre-filled your tax offset code for you based on the information you entered into myTax.
At Personalise return , you don’t need to make a selection to show seniors and pensioners as it's always displayed at Prepare return when you:
- have shown an amount at Australian Government payments such as pensions and carer payments
- are 60 years old or older.
To show your seniors and pensioners tax offset code, at Prepare return select 'Add/Edit' at the Offsets banner.
At the Seniors and pensioners (includes self-funded retirees) heading:
- Has the Tax offset code pre-filled in myTax? Yes – go to step 2 . No – go to step 3 .
- Is the pre-filled code correct for your circumstances? If unsure, check Tax offset codes table. Yes – go to step 4 . No – go to step 3 .
- If you meet both of the eligibility conditions , choose the tax offset code that applies to you in the Tax offset codes table.
- Were you or your spouse a veteran, war widow or war widower? No – go to step 5 . Yes – choose the veteran code that applies to you in the Veteran codes table. Go to step 5 .
- Go back to myTax, which should be open in another tab or window. Select your Tax offset code and Veteran code (if applicable) from the drop-down lists.
- Select Save and continue when you have completed the Offsets section.
### Tax offset codes
Work out which of the following SAPTO code letters applied to your circumstances at any time during 2024–25.
Your eligibility and circumstance at any time in 2024–25 | Code
You met the eligibility conditions for this tax offset and were single, separated or widowed. | A
You and your spouse: both met the eligibility conditions for this tax offset, and had to live apart due to illness or live apart because one of you was in a nursing home. | B
You met the eligibility conditions for this tax offset, but your spouse didn't, and you and your spouse either had to live apart due to illness live apart because one of you was in a nursing home. | C
You and your spouse: lived together, and both met the eligibility conditions for this tax offset. | D
You and your spouse: lived together, and you met the eligibility conditions for this tax offset, but your spouse didn't. | E
If more than one code applies, use the first code in the list above that applies to you, except:
- if both A and B apply, use code A if your spouse's taxable income was $18,334 or more. Otherwise, use code B .
- if both A and D apply, use code A if your spouse's taxable income was $12,494 or more. Otherwise, use code D .
### Veteran codes
If you or your spouse is a veteran, war widow or war widowers, select the veteran code letters that applies to you.
Circumstance | Code letter
You're a veteran, war widow or war widower. | V
Your spouse was a veteran, war widow or war widower. | W
Both you and your spouse were a veteran, war widow or war widower. | X
## Eligibility conditions
To claim this offset, you must meet condition 1 and condition 2. If you meet both conditions, go to Completing this section .
### Eligibility for Australian Government pensions and similar payments
Condition 1: You meet this condition if you can answer Yes to any of the following in 2024–25.
Requirement | Do you meet this requirement?
At Australian Government payments such as pensions and carer payments in your tax return, you included either: a pension or allowance from Centrelink a pension, allowance or benefit from the Department of Veterans' Affairs (DVA). Visit Australian Government payments such as pensions and carer payments to learn more. Note: If you're under the age-pension age : Australian Government payments aren't taxable in some circumstances. When these payments aren't taxable, don't include them at Australian Government payments such as pensions and carer payments . If your Australian Government payments aren't taxable, they don't meet this eligibility condition. Visit Amounts that you do not pay tax on to learn more. | Yes/No
You met the Centrelink age pension age requirement and were eligible for an Australian Government age pension during 2024–25, but didn't receive it because: you didn't make a claim you didn't meet the income test or the assets test, but you satisfied one of the following you had been an Australian resident for age-pension purposes for 10 years or more, of which 5 years of these years were continuous you had a qualifying residence exemption because you arrived in Australia as a refugee or under a special humanitarian program you received a partner allowance, a widow allowance or a widow B pension, immediately before turning age pension age you're a woman who was widowed in Australia, and you and your late partner were Australian residents when your late partner died you're currently in Australia you have been an Australian resident for at least the last 2 years you're a woman and on 19 March 2020 you were either receiving a wife pension and you weren't receiving a carer allowance; or a special needs widow B pension you're a woman and on 31 December 2021 you were receiving a widow allowance you would have qualified for an age pension under an international social security agreement. | Yes/No
You met the veteran pension age test and were eligible for a pension, allowance or benefit from Veterans' Affairs during 2024–25, but didn't receive it because: you didn't make a claim, or you didn't meet the income test or the assets test, and you're either a veteran with eligible war service a Commonwealth veteran, allied veteran or allied mariner with qualifying service. | Yes/No
If you:
- answer Yes to any of the condition 1 requirements, continue to condition 2 .
- answer No to all the condition 1 requirements, you're not eligible for this tax offset.
#### Centrelink age pension age
To be eligible for an Australian Government age pension from Centrelink, on 30 June 2025 you must be 67 years or older External Link .
To work out whether you're eligible for a social security or Centrelink pension only, go to the Services Australia website External Link or contact Services Australia External Link .
#### Veteran pension age test
To be eligible for a pension, allowance or benefit from Veterans' Affairs you must:
- be 60 years old or older on 30 June 2025, and
- meet the veteran pension age test.
You meet the veteran pension age test if one of the following applied to you, and you're eligible for a pension, allowance or benefit under the Veterans' Entitlements Act 1986 :
- you have eligible war service, that is, service in World War II or operational service as a member of the Australian Defence Force
- you're a Commonwealth or allied veteran who served in a conflict in which the Australian Defence Force was engaged during a period of hostilities that is, World War II, Korea, Malaya, Indonesia or Vietnam
- you're an Australian or allied mariner who served during World War II
- you're the war widow or widower of a former member of the Australian Defence Force.
Pension, allowance or benefit includes:
- disability pension
- service pension
- white or gold Repatriation health cards for treatment entitlements.
If you're not sure whether you're eligible for a payment, go to the Department of Veterans' Affairs website External Link or contact the Department of Veterans' Affairs External Link .
### Rebate income amounts
Condition 2: You meet this condition if you can answer Yes to any of the following for 2024–25.
Requirement | Do you meet this requirement?
You didn't have a spouse, and your rebate income was less than $52,759. | Yes/No
You had a spouse, and the combined rebate income of you and your spouse was less than $87,620. | Yes/No
At any time in 2024–25 both of the following apply you and your spouse had to live apart due to illness or because one of you was in a nursing home the combined rebate income of you and your spouse was less than $100,104. | Yes/No
If you:
- answer Yes to any of the condition 2 requirements, go to Completing this section .
- answer No to all the condition 2 requirements, you're not eligible for this tax offset.
#### Rebate income
Your ‘rebate income' is used to determine whether you're eligible for the seniors and pensioners tax offset.
If you have a spouse, your combined rebate income is used to work out whether you're eligible for this tax offset, but your own rebate income is used to work out the amount you're entitled to. Depending on how much income you have earned, you could meet the overall eligibility requirements and still not get this tax offset.
Use the following worksheet if you want to know how to work out your:
- rebate income
- combined rebate income (if you have a spouse).
Row | Calculation | You | Spouse
a | Taxable income | $ | $
b | Assessable first home super saver (FHSS) released amount | $ | $
c | Take away the amount at row b from a . If the amount is less than zero, write 0 at row c . The result is your taxable income disregarding your assessable FHSS released amount. | $ | $
d | Reportable employer superannuation contributions | $ | $
e | Deductible personal superannuation contributions | $ | $
f | Net financial investment loss | $ | $
g | Net rental property loss | $ | $
h | Reportable fringe benefits amounts from employers exempt from FBT under section 57A of FBTAA 1986 multiplied by 0.53. Round down the answer to the nearest dollar. | $ | $
i | Reportable fringe benefits amounts from employers not exempt from FBT under section 57A of FBTAA 1986. | $ | $
j | Add up the amounts from rows c to i . This is each individual's rebate income. | $ | $
k | The amount on which a trustee of a trust was liable to pay tax in respect of your spouse because your spouse was under a legal disability, such as: being an undischarged bankrupt a person who was declared legally incapable because of a mental condition. | N/A | $
l | Add the amount from: row j in your column row j in your spouse's column, and row k in your spouse's column. This is your combined rebate income. | $ | $
